speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is December the 11th, 1996. The survivor's name is Regina Mauskopf. The interviewer is Gustav Goldberger. The place is Bethseda, Maryland, United States. And the language is English. Today's date is December the 11th, 1996. The survivor's name is Regina Mauskopf. The interviewer is Gustav Goldberger. Place is Bethseda, Maryland, the United States. And the language is English. Good afternoon. What is your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina Mauskopf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regina Helman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Mauskopf, I want to thank you on behalf of the Shoah Foundation for allowing us to do this interview. Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Solocin, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's July 13th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time, were you living in a particular home that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Nathan Helman. And my mother's maiden name was Rose [? Klineman ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were nine-- nine children-- eight siblings. I was the ninth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you quickly or briefly summarize the names of your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The oldest is Yosl, and Yankel and [? Freida, ?] and [INAUDIBLE], and [? Mil ?], and Malka, [INAUDIBLE], and Lesier.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the siblings, what were the ranges of age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have-- the oldest was like 1895 or '96. Then Yankel was born like two-- two years after. And [? Freida ?] was, I think, [? 1900, ?] I think. And then it was [INAUDIBLE] in '91, '92. And then was [? Mil, ?] but-- I mean, 190--

speaker: interviewer
text: Two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three or four. And then Malka was born on 1909. And [INAUDIBLE] was born in 1911, and Leiser in 1913.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Solocin-- that's how you pronounce it?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Solechin.'

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Solechin.' When you were growing up in Solocin, can you kind of describe what life was like in Solocin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was not too many-- just about 11 Jewish families. And life was very nice. It's country thing. There were-- one of-- one of the Jewish people had a grocery store. And that's all, what it was. And we were very happy, like really happy. It was-- everybody had fields. And they had the-- the necessary things like was from-- from the-- like-- it's not like farmers. But on the smaller range, everybody had their own [? farm-- ?] [? I know ?] from on year to another whatever was grown in the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you consider yourself a-- a farmer's daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- I was doing the work, especially in the war when it came. There was nobody to hire, because everybody was gone. The brothers were gone, which they use to come and help. So I did a lot of work with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was a foreman by deforestation. I don't know how to explain that, the how--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lumber?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lumber, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lumber business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lumber business, yeah. He worked not for himself, but for-- he was like employed by somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say there were about 11 Jewish families.

speaker: interviewee
text: Families, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the entire community?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole village?

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole village.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a village. It's not even [? state-like. ?] It's just one street. The whole village was like close to 200 houses. But as I say, a lot of people lived like two families. The-- the peasants lived like two families the-- in the-- in a house. So they was [INAUDIBLE] kind of like the Jewish people, where everybody was separate in one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the house you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should have shown. I have a kind of like doodle [INAUDIBLE] when the children were small in a picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's kind of-- it's like the log cabins, something like that. But the logs were not out. They were painted white, kind of like they do some places. They make-- some people make blue. Some people make pink. Ours was kind of like the white. But it was like cabin-like, because we had right beside it, we have a stable. We had always three cows, because for milk and butter to have. And-- and not everybody had-- well, the most everybody. We were just kind of two families, which the rest of the village took care.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many bedrooms did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is kind of like one. In those small villages, it's like this. It's one bedroom-- one bathroom, a big one, which there was the oven to bake bread, like the-- like the Indian style with the thing and the-- and to cook, the stove built in. And there were beds. And there was a big table. It was big.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then was another room. Now, they were sleeping like two together, sometimes three. And no if company came, they put them-- well, in Yiddish you say, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. You know? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how it was. In the summertime, there was in the stable fresh hay, or even outside. Then-- and-- it was really-- we had a creek right in the front of the house. We had a lot of pear trees and apple trees and around and walnuts they up. There was a little hill up. And we had a lot of field like right after the house and then separately, which was for hay to cut for the winter for the-- for the animals. And so it was we had a creek, as I said, right in the front of the house. In the summertime, we used to see about like when we didn't have any chores to do, we're sitting there and thinking. Kept our feet in the water to cool ourselves off. And we, the girls, we were doing embroideries, knitting, crocheting, whatever it had to be done. And it was [INAUDIBLE]. Sometimes there was a little bridge. And the-- the water was a little bit deeper than over the knees. And we just in the afternoon just jumped in and-- well, couldn't swim but just dunk in in the cool water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have happy memories of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes, very, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about religious practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the religious practice, well, there were boys. And the boys got a [INAUDIBLE]. That we called the [INAUDIBLE], the teacher. He was the teacher. And it was like from after Pesach till before the Rosh Hashanah. And then after the Sukkot, there was high-- or the same or-- you know, or somebody else. They went to the city. And there was the-- the looking over the things and then through the winter. And it was like this, that whoever had a boy, a child, everybody-- they divided the weeks, how many weeks and how many children. So in every house, the teacher was in that family house, room and board. If there were two-- two children, they had to-- to do a double. They figured it out between themselves. And the most-- the boys were going. The girls were not-- they were-- the girls was like this. We get picked up, whatever it is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My girlfriend went to a special-- not special. I mean, she had [? their ?] two brothers were kind of in the yeshiva. When they were home, they were teaching her [INAUDIBLE]. Because they were kind of younger, she-- they were still at home. Well, when I was home, my brothers were not already. They were-- well, one of them, which lived in Prague. And the other one was working away. So I surprised my parents with a Hebrew reading. Well, we had geese and things. So I used to go and mind the geese way out from the village. Because as I say, it's just kind of country life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I use to switch in the [INAUDIBLE], and my, whatever I took lunch. And I learned to read. And I surprised my parents one day when I knew how to read. And they were very happy. And that's how it was. Was there a synagogue or a beth midrash in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a beth midrash. It was kind of just one of the people had. And they just didn't have any children. They had one son, kind of, grown up-- in my time, what I remember. I don't remember something way before. And he donated like half of the house. He left for himself two bedrooms and the two of them. So one was for the women fixed and the other one was for the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: They closed up-- I mean, opened the wall. And they made the small, little window kind of just the women should know where the davening is-- what would you call it? Don't know how to say it in English-- you know, the [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE] the minyan, the davening. And [INAUDIBLE] was just two of them. It was my girlfriend's father and [INAUDIBLE] and my father. Them two just could read the Torah. So it was an Orthodox home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, real Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the closest large-- larger city near the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [INAUDIBLE] was [INAUDIBLE] where we went to-- to buy the other necessary things for the house, and like your clothing could be too, and the smaller thing. But for the real one is [? Mukacheve. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was [? Mukacheve, ?] approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately, I-- I forgot about how much the kilometers. It took like that. We had to go to the train like an hour and a half and then to [INAUDIBLE], and that just by walking. And from [INAUDIBLE] took an hour for the train. In between was little villages just kind of thing, though. But it took an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it-- it was quite a distance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get along with your-- your non-Jewish neighbors, if you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. Well, I had girlfriends, a lot of them. And as I said, they were very nice. And I specially when was kind of with them. Not just me, there were-- a lot of the youngsters were kind of-- they-- we were like-- but there was. There was something. Like for instance, when the children were small. When they were bigger, that's OK. But you know when children are small, I don't know, in Yiddish, we have a-- a have a saying. I don't know if I should say it.

words.text: know if I should say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can say it in Yiddish. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are saying, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. You know what it mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: What they hear in the house, then they blurt out when the time comes. So as I was playing, you know, when we were real little with the Gentile children, if they got angry, they started calling me names. And they said, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that means in Russian, Jews to Palestine. Oh, then in a minute, we were you best friend again. But it was there. But the-- and the-- the bigger people, I mean the grownups, were very nice. Yeah. We had a lot of Gentile friends in the village. Our neighbors were really good. And we had a sheriff in the-- in the village. That was the highest of like from the government and no police. From the-- the police were from the second village thing. So we were really very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sheriff you say was a friendly person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ivan Semik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there ever any situations when he helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the time came like that he knew the situation from-- from the higher quarters that we're going to be taken away, he suggested once that he could hide three, four people. So it was [INAUDIBLE] between ourselves kind of. He knew approximately that which was, you know, like I had a very girlfriend. The rest of the boys were away on that forced labor. You know, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Forced labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, things. And so he said-- so we [INAUDIBLE]. He told that for my father. And then we talked it over in the house. And my parents said like this. We are old. And we'll go together. We stay together. Maybe you're gonna be a help for them. Because the rest of them were all out of the house. I was the only one when the-- in that time. So that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he-- this sheriff you say was a very helpful person at some point later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then when the-- then we left already from the house, everything was boarded up. Inside the house, was-- I myself took the cows like a few days before. There was a certain place. And all the Jewish people had to bring their-- whoever had cows. The-- the chickens, and the geese, and the ducks, that was left to whatever. They were roaming around in the-- in the yard or in the backyard or wherever. But those, they took away. And I remember that. I myself went there and helped my father, well, mother. Well, I was always helping out from my father because that's what-- mother was in the house. And so when they started to open the house and to confiscate whatever was useful for them from the house, the furniture and clothing, whatever, valuables, whatever, well, we tried to hide-- to give some for the other neighborhoods to hide. But it was very strict, because every-- every day, we didn't have like here at the radio and they announce on the radio the news, wherever it's going on in-- in the city of wherever. We had a town crier. In the good times, too, if something came up, news for the-- you know, that the village has to be repaired, something. In the good times, there was a town crier. That's what they call it, with a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A town crier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he was in a-- in a-- what is it called? Like the musicians have it, too. Gee. And so they were kind of warning the Gentile people that whoever is gonna take something to hide for the Jewish people, they are gonna be punished. So we hid. So they took out from the house everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you go on with that, if you can-- if I can bring you back earlier. When did you first become aware of special problems for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was in '30-- in '39 when the Hungarians came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say the Hungarians came in, how-- how did you make that observation? What-- what happened? Was it one day, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because they-- let me. I have to think about it. Forgotten all about it, how that was going. I remember when they came in. And they said that the Hungarians gonna come in, that won't be good for the Jews. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, after the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't do right away things. It just happened when the Germans really kind of-- well, they-- I-- I really don't know the dates and the politics, how it was then when the Germans took over the real Hungarians, and-- or it was-- I know in '39, the Hungarians came in. When the Germans took over the Hungarians, like made that [INAUDIBLE] with them, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when the Hungarians came in, how did that affect your daily life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was the same thing for a while. Because that was in '39. And in '44, they took us away. In-- in between, it was OK. They were really-- we knew that they are big anti-Semites. But they didn't bother. As a matter of fact, like the officials, they were very good with the Jewish people. Because the-- it's socialization kind of like. They didn't have, you know, like they came in there. So with the Jewish people-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no. Well, I put it that way, more than the peasants. Kind of-- it was kind of like appropriate with them-- to-- to socialize.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Nazis, let's say, in 1939 that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they were not in your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did-- when did it start getting bad for the Jews in your particular village? When did you feel that there was a problem with the Nazis or the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: That started the winter-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's see, make the date '42 they took us away. That was right in the beginning of '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that time when we started to wear-- I think that's when we start to wear the-- the-- the yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember putting on a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember having it on, but not exactly the day to-- when we put it on. Because I know that-- that-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No. It must have been in '41. Because it's kind of too close from the taking away. Because I know there was the Hungarians going to the front of Russia. And they stopped in our village. And we had a, I don't know, a high rank official in our house. We gave one of those bedrooms to give them, and the horses there, and the stable. And-- and my sister across the street, she lived. And in their house was, you know, [? all ?] [? the way. ?] They took in the Jewish places, because the Jewish people had the houses more to accommodate them and-- and the food. Well, they had their own kitchen. But they liked the home cooked meals. And they were very nice. But I know that we had already that time the-- the yellow star, because I had a sister living a little bit further [INAUDIBLE], because the gentleman what he gave the house, he moved to the city, to [? Mukacheve. ?] And my sister was living there by [INAUDIBLE]. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember like a Thursday night. My sister didn't [INAUDIBLE] to bake bread. And Thursday night, we had to prepare the bread for Friday morning to bake the challahs and the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was not allowed for me to go to my sister to get what she wants to ask if [INAUDIBLE], and especially after 6 o'clock, or whatever it was, the curfew, was not allowed. So the guy which was there, he was the-- he was cleaning the [INAUDIBLE] for that big captain, whatever. He stayed in our house. So he went with me. Because if he went with me, that means that I was allowed to go there. And that's where it start. It was kind of like about--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and they left. Like, they were kind of like a week or two they were resting there. And then they went towards the Polish [INAUDIBLE] border. And-- and he knew, too. He was throwing some words that big captain, or whatever it was, and not to worry. And he was saying goodbye. And he told for my parents there. I was in that room, in that second room. And when I heard what he was saying, I remember myself crying, stand there by the window. I have that little piece where I doodle sometimes right by that window. And I was crying, because it's nearing something. And so after that right away, it kind-- it was almost like that Pesach time coming. And we couldn't go to [INAUDIBLE] to that-- you know, like to buy things. And if you went, you did not go on the sidewalk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was-- that was because you were Jewish, and because you were wearing the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we had the star. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And every-- every Jew in the village had to wear a star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no exceptions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No exceptions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, from 1939 to-- to the time that you're now describing, was your-- was your father able to continue to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he retired from that place already, from that what he was doing, the foreman and managing that stuff. No, he did not work already at that time. So-- because he retired and was just kind of like home. He got a little pension from there. And the rest of it, we-- we mined the fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember whether the Jewish stores were allowed to keep open?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember that. I don't really remember. I should have, because I was at my girlfriend like [INAUDIBLE], and I don't know who.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this important person-- you call him captain or whatever-- he was Hungarian? Is that what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: From that military?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Hungarian. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number one.
